_comment: Source of truth for training-mode reasoning tags. v2 restructures the vocabulary around reasoning primitives (trump hand, non-trump hand, shape, bidding action, partner/opponent/score context, meta) so each tag is a boolean feature a bot could evaluate against hand + bidding state — prerequisite for rule extraction (annotations pair tag-sets with action values; extractor clusters by tag-set). Keys are kebab-case and IMMUTABLE once shipped (renaming corrupts stored annotations — deprecate and add instead). Display strings live in frontend/src/i18n/{en,fr}.js under training.tags.groups.<group-key> and training.tags.<action>.<tag-key>. `description` is developer documentation only. Group-level `requireExactlyOne` and `recommendAtLeastOne` flags drive validator rules. Tag-level `requiresNote: true` means the note must be non-empty when that tag is present. bidding-action is required (exactly one tag per decision); trump-hand is recommended (warning, not error). play-card is carried over unchanged from v1 pending a dedicated v2 revision — its legacy groups (hand-claim, tactical, partner-signal, defensive, situational, uncertainty, other) coexist with the v2 groups. v1 is archived at reasonTags.v1.json.

tags.description: Player holds J, 9, and A of trump (all three).
J of trump + exactly 1 other trump. Total trump = 2.
J of trump + exactly 2 other trumps. Total trump = 3.
J of trump + exactly 3 other trumps. Total trump = 4.
J of trump + exactly 4 other trumps. Total trump = 5.
9 of trump + 1 other trump, AND no J of trump. Total trump = 2.
9 of trump + 2 other trumps, no J. Total = 3.
9 of trump + 3 other trumps, no J. Total = 4.
9 of trump + 4 other trumps, no J. Total = 5.
Exactly 2 trump cards total. Tag alongside the matching pattern tag (valet-second / 9-second) when applicable.
Exactly 3 trump cards total.
Exactly 4 trump cards total.
5 or more trump cards.
K and Q of trump both in hand.
Zero Aces in non-trump suits.
Exactly 1 Ace in non-trump suits.
Exactly 2 Aces in non-trump suits.
Exactly 3 Aces in non-trump suits.
2 non-trump Aces AND no meaningful trump strength (no J, no 9, ≤1 trump total). The 80-informatif pattern.
A and 10 of the same non-trump suit.
21 pattern in two different non-trump suits.
In a single non-trump suit: A, 10, and K all present. Minimum 3 cards of that suit.
Hand concentrated in exactly 2 suits (each of the other 2 suits has 0–1 cards). One of the 2 suits becomes trump if player bids.
Hand contains exactly 1 non-trump card the player judges will likely lose a trick unpredictably — the trick-losing moment can’t be controlled or anticipated.
Same criterion as fausse-carte-1, count = 2.
First bid of the auction (all previous players passed or player is first seat).
Raises previous bid, same suit.
Raises previous bid, different suit.
Bid primarily to block opponents from reaching their ideal level.
Deliberately overbids to provoke opponents into a coinche-able overbid.
Bid whose primary purpose is information signal to partner, not claim.
Genuine raise based on real hand strength, intending to play the contract.
Player is first seat, no prior bids.
Partner’s first non-pass bid was 80.
Partner’s first non-pass bid was 90.
Partner’s first non-pass bid was 100.
Partner’s first non-pass bid was 110 or higher.
Partner’s prior bid uses the suit player is considering.
Partner’s prior bid uses a different suit from player’s consideration.
An opponent made the first bid of the auction.
An opponent raised a previous bid.
Teams within ~100 pts of each other, game not near threshold.
Player’s team is behind; conservative play won’t catch up; justifies stretching.
Player’s team is ahead; favor safer contracts.
Final hand of the match; bid tuned to exact points needed.
Decision was judgment-based, can’t fully articulate in tags/rules.
Player unsure decision was correct. Review after hand plays out.
None of the above tags capture the reasoning. Requires non-empty note. Gap-detection signal. Also used for coinche/surcoinche reasoning until a sub-vocabulary is designed.

None of the above tags capture the reasoning. Requires non-empty note. Gap-detection signal. Also used for coinche/surcoinche reasoning until a sub-vocabulary is designed.
Playing my winner while the suit is live — opponents haven't cut it yet.

Leading trump myself to flush opponents' trump out of the way. Paired with forcing-opponent-to-trump — drawing-trump is leading trump; forcing-opponent-to-trump is leading a NON-trump into a known void to make an opponent spend a trump.

Playing so partner's remaining cards become master in this suit. Paired with letting-partner-win — promoting is about making partner's FUTURE cards master; letting-partner-win is about giving up THIS specific trick.

Deliberately playing too low to win even though I could, letting partner take this trick instead. Paired with promoting-partners-card — that one is about future tricks in a suit. Paired with non-default-winner-choice — that one is about WHICH winner when winning; letting-partner-win is about whether to win at all.

Card choice communicates which suit partner should lead back. Paired with appel-direct and appel-indirect — those are specific conventions (lead-based and discard-based); signalling-suit-to-partner is the general case when neither specific convention fits.

K/Q order when declaring Belote signals trump count to partner. Q-first declaration = 3 or more trumps (Q, K, and at least one more). K-first declaration = exactly 2 trumps (just K+Q). Fires only when declaring Belote. Active convention signal — not a byproduct of which card is playable.

Leading a non-top card in a suit to request partner return it when they next win. Signal: I'm not cashing the top; I'm asking for access to partner's higher holding in this suit. Typical case is a middling non-trump card.

Communicating suit preference via discard — when forced to dump off-suit, the suit I throw away carries information ('not this suit'), pointing partner toward another suit by elimination. Paired with appel-direct — direct is a lead-based request, indirect is a discard-based one.
Holding my A/10/trump-J so it doesn't get trumped or stolen.
Not trumping now because a higher-value trick later needs this trump.

Shedding a valueless card when I can't win or contribute. Paired with shedding-to-create-ruff — dumping-garbage has no strategic intent; shedding-to-create-ruff is an engineered void to set up a future trump.
No choice — rules force this card (must follow, must overtrump, only card left).

Deliberately picked a different winner than the obvious one — either bigger (for points) or smaller (to save value). The direction is recoverable from game state; the tag flags that the choice was deliberate. Paired with letting-partner-win — non-default-winner-choice is about WHICH winner when winning; letting-partner-win is about not winning at all.

Deliberately voiding a suit now so I can trump it on a later lead. Paired with dumping-garbage — dumping-garbage is shedding without strategic intent; shedding-to-create-ruff is the engineered void that sets up a future trump trick.

Leading a suit I know a specific opponent is void in, to force them to spend a trump. Inference-driven — relies on known voids from prior tricks or the auction. Paired with drawing-trump — drawing-trump is leading trump myself to flush theirs; forcing-opponent-to-trump is leading a non-trump to make them burn one of theirs.

Tricks 6-7 specifically — trump counts known, short hands, positioning for dix-de-der. Distinct decision mode from mid-round play. Paired with last-trick-dix-de-der — endgame-positioning covers the lead-up, last-trick-dix-de-der fires only on trick 8.
Playing for the +10 dix-de-der on the final trick. Paired with endgame-positioning — dix-de-der is trick 8; endgame-positioning is tricks 6-7.
Two plays were defensible; picked one.
Playing on feel; flag for review.
No existing tag fits — note required.
